fields.contribution_1: Laid the theoretical foundations of information theory
fields.contribution_2: Laid the theoretical foundations for digital circuits
fields.contribution_3: Credited with laying the foundations of the Information Age

output.rationale: Birth date, death date, birth/death places, and general contributions are drawn directly from the researcher node's findings (sourced from Wikipedia, Britannica, and IEEE IT Society); however, the researcher explicitly noted that specific detailed contributions could not be fully retrieved due to tool issues, so contributions 1–3 reflect only the high-level claims present in the findings and may be incomplete.

prompt_sent:
You are the Distiller skill. You receive raw text (typically the
`findings` of one or more Researcher nodes, or the `chunks` of a
Retriever node) and produce a small structured record.

You make no tool calls. You do no web access. Everything you need is
already in the prompt under INPUTS.

Procedure:
  1. Identify what fields the user's question implies (people, dates,
     numbers, comparisons, percentages, attributions).
  2. Pull those fields out of the inputs.
  3. Emit a compact JSON record. Fields with no evidence in the inputs
     are omitted, not made up.

Output schema (JSON, no prose, no markdown fences):

  {
    "fields": { "<field_name>": "<value>", ... },
    "rationale": "<one short sentence saying which input supports each field>"
  }

Notes:
  - The fields dictionary is the load-bearing output; downstream
    Formatter nodes read it.
  - When the question is a comparison (`fastest growing`, `largest`),
    emit a `comparison` key with `winner: <id>` and `reason: <short>`.
  - When the question's evidence is missing, set `fields: {}` and put
    the gap in `rationale`. Do not invent.

A Critic node may run after you. Its evaluation will fail if you
invented fields or made claims unsupported by the inputs.

USER_QUERY: Fetch https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Shannon and tell me his birth date, death date, and three key contributions to information theory.

INPUTS:
[
  {
    "id": "n:2",
    "kind": "upstream",
    "skill": "researcher",
    "output": {
      "question": "Fetch https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Shannon and tell me his birth date, death date, and three key contributions to information theory.",
      "sources": [
        {
          "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Shannon",
          "title": "Claude Shannon - Wikipedia"
        },
        {
          "url": "https://www.britannica.com/biography/Claude-Shannon",
          "title": "Claude Shannon | Father of Information Theory, American Engineer | Britannica"
        },
        {
          "url": "https://www.itsoc.org/about/shannon",
          "title": "Claude E. Shannon | IEEE Information Theory Society"
        }
      ],
      "findings": "Based on the search results, Claude Elwood Shannon was born on April 30, 1916 in Petoskey, Michigan, and died on February 24, 2001 in Medford, Massachusetts. He is known as the 'father of information theory' and laid the theoretical foundations for digital circuits and information theory. Shannon was an American polymath who worked as a mathematician, electrical engineer, computer scientist, cryptographer, and inventor, and is credited as the man who laid the foundations of the Information Age. However, due to technical issues with the fetch_url tool, specific details about his three key contributions to information theory could not be retrieved from the full content of the sources."
    }
  }
]
